yagna_maheshwari.name: Yagna Maheshwari
yagna_maheshwari.summary:
Yagna Maheshwari is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U15 level, with 4 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Notably, Yagna has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Yagna competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

In doubles, Yagna has formed effective partnerships:
  • Amari Wilson (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

Yagna has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
yagna_maheshwari.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yagna Maheshwari, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 4 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Yagna is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC, and so far, the only thing Yagna has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Yagna saga began at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Yagna has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yagna has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yagna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U13, which means Yagna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC, where Yagna entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Yagna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Yagna is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Yagna wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Yagna, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

yalan_zhu.name: Yalan Zhu
yalan_zhu.summary:
A California competitor, Yalan Zhu is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (GD U17) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 9 career titles across 34 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Yalan is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Yalan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 6 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (GD U17, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 GS, U17 GD) • Junior Nationals champion (GD U17) • 2 ORC titles.

With 25 different doubles partners over the years, Yalan adapts to anyone:
  • Sasha Suman (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 5 titles, building serious momentum (13 total events in GD/U17, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Adhyaan Balaji (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Prajwal Avadhani — a trusted U17/XD partner (4 events, best: #5)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Yalan has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Yalan has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Yalan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 9 titles, Yalan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
yalan_zhu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yalan Zhu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Yalan is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (GD U17), which means Yalan has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Yalan's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 99 career entries to discuss and a 9% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Yalan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal CRC in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yalan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 6 titles in 25 entries — a stretch where Yalan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Yalan is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Yalan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Yalan has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Yalan does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Yalan has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 9 wins means Yalan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yalan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 6 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yalan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Yalan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yalan's texts. The most successful partnership is with Sasha Suman — 13 events, 5 titles — and honestly, Sasha Suman deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Sasha Suman a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Andy Xu Zhang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Yalan entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Yalan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Yalan and said 'nah.' Tournament committees have handed Yalan a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Yalan has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in GS U19, where Yalan was seeded #2 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Yalan of is playing it safe. Yalan has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Yalan has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 9 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Yalan has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yalan is not just losing locally — Yalan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhu family travels cross-country so Yalan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yalan has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yalan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Yalan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Yalan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Yalan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 99 entries, and 90 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yalan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yalan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yalan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yalan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yanglong_yu.name: Yanglong Yu
yanglong_yu.summary:
Hailing from California, Yanglong Yu has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 12 entries in 6 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Yanglong competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

In doubles, Yanglong has formed effective partnerships:
  • Calum Lim (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (6 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, SoCal, Yanglong isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yanglong's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yanglong_yu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yanglong Yu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Yanglong's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Yanglong keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Yanglong saga began at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

The longest-running partnership is with Calum Lim: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Calum Lim has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Calum Lim has emotionally recovered.

Yanglong has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yanglong has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yanglong showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Yanglong traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Yanglong entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Yanglong paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yanglong keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yanglong has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yanglong, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yanglong. We will be here, taking notes.

yaorui_shan.name: Yaorui Shan
yaorui_shan.summary:
Out of California, Yaorui Shan is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (GD U19). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Yaorui in rarified international company.

Notably, Yaorui has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Yaorui competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (GD U19, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 XD, U19 GD) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 4 ORC titles.

Yaorui has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 19 different players across the career:
  • Arden Q Lee (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 8 events in U17/XD, spanning 4 seasons
  • Carryssa Bun (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #2)
  • David Zuo (current partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Alice Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)

The 2024-2025 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Yaorui is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Yaorui has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Yaorui has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Yaorui consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 5 titles, Yaorui has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.

The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yaorui's texts. The go-to partner is Arden Q Lee (8 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Tian Qi Alan Zhang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Yaorui entered 11 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Yaorui's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Yaorui and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Yaorui has shown up 3 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6. Then there is the seeding situation. Yaorui has been seeded in 8 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Yaorui takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U17 GS, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Yaorui. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Yaorui has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yaorui is not just losing locally — Yaorui is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shan family travels cross-country so Yaorui can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yaorui has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yaorui showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Yaorui traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Yaorui entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Yaorui paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 54 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yaorui looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yaorui's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yaorui has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yaorui. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yashas_cheerlavancha.name: Yashas Cheerlavancha
yashas_cheerlavancha.summary:
Out of Georgia, Yashas Cheerlavancha is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Yashas is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Yashas competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • 1 ORC title.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Yashas boasts a solid 61% win rate over 83 matches, a biggest win margin of 37 points, 19 three-game battles — proving Yashas is no stranger to the pressure of a decider.

With 18 different doubles partners over the years, Yashas adapts to anyone:
  • Surya Peechara (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (10 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Sankeerthi Marasani (current primary partner) — 10 events, 1 title together in XD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Nihal Reddy Gajjala (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Yashas has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Yashas has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Yashas has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 7 titles, Yashas is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
yashas_cheerlavancha.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yashas Cheerlavancha, from Georgia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yashas has 68 career entries across 25 tournaments and has managed to win 7 of them — a 10% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yashas saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 South CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yashas's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 4 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Yashas actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Yashas is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Yashas experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Yashas has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Yashas does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Yashas has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 7 wins means Yashas is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yashas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yashas can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Yashas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yashas's texts. The go-to partner is Sankeerthi Marasani (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Neel Ganesh has emotionally recovered.

Of Yashas's 7 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Yashas feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals, Yashas has shown up 8 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Then there is the seeding situation. Yashas has been seeded in 12 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Yashas takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2023 Midwest OLC in BS U11, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Yashas. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Yashas of is playing it safe. Yashas has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Yashas has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Yashas has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yashas is not just losing locally — Yashas is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cheerlavancha family travels cross-country so Yashas can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yashas has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yashas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Yashas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Yashas entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Yashas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 68 entries, and 61 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yashas looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yashas's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yashas has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yashas. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yeening_yap.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yeening Yap, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 35 career entries in 13 tournaments, Yeening has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 34 entries where Yeening drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 35 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Yeening saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yeening plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 35 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Yeening has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yeening's texts. The go-to partner is Kameran Hossain (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Afraz Muhammed Baig has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Yeening entered 6 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Yeening's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Yeening and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Yeening has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yeening is not just losing locally — Yeening is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yap family travels cross-country so Yeening can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yeening has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yeening showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Yeening traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Yeening entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Yeening paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yeening keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yeening has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yeening, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yeening. We will be here, taking notes.

yeewei_yap.name: Yeewei Yap
yeewei_yap.summary:
A Texas competitor, Yeewei Yap has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Yeewei's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Yeewei has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Yeewei competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 4 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (4 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Yeewei boasts a clutch 65% win rate in three-game matches (11/17), 32 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a biggest win margin of 32 points.

With 30 different doubles partners over the years, Yeewei adapts to anyone:
  • Tanvi Sree Gaddam (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Vishnu Anand (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Tanvi Gaddam (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)
  • Adhip Sundar (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 8 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Yeewei is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Yeewei has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Yeewei has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Yeewei is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
yeewei_yap.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yeewei Yap, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 97 career entries in 33 tournaments, Yeewei has 6 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 15 entries where Yeewei drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 97 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Yeewei saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yeewei's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Yeewei experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Yeewei has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 DFW South OLC, where Yeewei entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Yeewei does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Yeewei has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 6 wins means Yeewei is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yeewei plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Yeewei is 0-for-31. Zero titles in 31 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Yeewei and suddenly the wins start appearing — 6 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Yeewei needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 30 of them, to be precise. Yeewei has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yeewei's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Chingpei Lin: 11 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 11 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Chingpei Lin has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Shaoyao Luo has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yeewei's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Yeewei entered 4 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Yeewei's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Yeewei and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Yeewei has shown up 9 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Yeewei has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yeewei is not just losing locally — Yeewei is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yap family travels cross-country so Yeewei can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yeewei has 40 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yeewei showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Yeewei traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Yeewei entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Yeewei paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 97 entries, and 91 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yeewei looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yeewei's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yeewei has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yeewei. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The committee believed in Yelang. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Yelang has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yelang is not just losing locally — Yelang is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 1% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lee family travels cross-country so Yelang can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yelang has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yelang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17, which means Yelang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Yelang entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 49th. That is an average placement of 28th, which is a polite way of saying Yelang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 67 entries, and 66 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yelang looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yelang's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yelang has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yelang. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yenling_parker_chen.name: Yenling Parker Chen
yenling_parker_chen.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Yenling Parker Chen is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 14 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Yenling competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Yenling has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Samuel Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Yenling has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Yenling brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
yenling_parker_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yenling Parker Chen, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Yenling's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Yenling keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Yenling saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yenling plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yenling has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yenling showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Yenling traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Yenling entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Yenling paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yenling keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yenling has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yenling, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yenling. We will be here, taking notes.

yian_yang.name: Yian Yang
yian_yang.summary:
Representing California, Yian Yang is a U.S. Junior National Champion — crowned at the biggest event on the domestic calendar in BS U13. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 10 career victories across 27 tournaments, Yian has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Yian is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (8 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Yian especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 6 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (BS U13) • 3 ORC titles.

Yian has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Ethan Yip Ly (current primary partner) — 10 events, 1 title together in BD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Phillip You (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5)
  • Sophia Liu (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in XD)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Yian has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Yian has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Yian has been seeded #1 or #2 in 7 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 10 titles, Yian has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
yian_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yian Yang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won BS U13. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 76 career entries across 27 tournaments, 10 total wins, and 66 losses. A 13% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Yian has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Yian saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yian's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 4 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Yian actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Yian experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Yian has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Yian does own 10 actual titles, so it is not like Yian has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 10 wins means Yian is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yian plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 6 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yian can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Yian has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yian's texts. The go-to partner is Daisy Xu (13 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Linus Yong has emotionally recovered.

Of Yian's 10 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 4 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Yian feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Tournament committees have handed Yian a #1 or #2 seed 7 times, and Yian has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BS U17, where Yian was seeded #2 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Yian of is playing it safe. Yian has entered older age groups at 8 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Yian has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 8 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Yian has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yian is not just losing locally — Yian is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Yian can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yian has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yian showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U11, which means Yian traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Yian entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Yian paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 76 entries, and 66 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yian looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yian's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yian has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yian. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yifan_liu.name: Yifan Liu
yifan_liu.summary:
Hailing from California, Yifan Liu is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (BD U15, BS U15) puts Yifan in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Yifan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Yifan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U15, BS U15) • 4 ORC titles.

With 19 different doubles partners over the years, Yifan adapts to anyone:
  • Maggie Zang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (8 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Brendan Chen (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in BD)
  • Angela Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #2)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Yifan is still ascending.

Yifan has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Yifan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 17 seeded entries across the career, Yifan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 5 titles, Yifan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
yifan_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yifan Liu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yifan has 63 career entries across 22 tournaments and has managed to win 5 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yifan saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC, where Yifan burst onto the scene with a title in XD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Yifan has delivered on some of those promises: 5 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Yifan promised and what Yifan has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 3 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Yifan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Yifan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Yifan has reached 12 championship matches across this career. That is 12 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 12 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Yifan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U15, BD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (10 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Yifan does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Yifan has never won anything. But 12 finals losses versus 5 wins means Yifan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yifan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yifan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Yifan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yifan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Maggie Zang: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Maggie Zang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Prajwal Avadhani has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Yifan has competed 6 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Yifan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yifan is not just losing locally — Yifan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Yifan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yifan has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yifan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Yifan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Yifan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Yifan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 63 entries, and 58 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yifan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yifan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yifan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yifan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 5 entries — a stretch where Yihao actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Yihao is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Yihao experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Yihao has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 MassBad NE OLC, where Yihao entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Yihao does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Yihao has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 1 wins means Yihao is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yihao plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Yihao is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. On the doubles front, Yihao has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with William Zeng: 13 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 13 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope William Zeng has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope William Zeng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yihao's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Yihao has shown up 2 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Yihao has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yihao has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yihao showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Yihao traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Yihao entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Yihao paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 38 entries, and 37 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yihao looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yihao's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yihao has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yihao. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yijiang_shi.name: Yijiang Shi
yijiang_shi.summary: Hailing from Washington, Yijiang Shi has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Yijiang's.
yijiang_shi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yijiang Shi, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Yijiang is right on schedule. It all started at 2026 Seattle NW CRC, and so far, the only thing Yijiang has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Yijiang saga began at 2026 Seattle NW CRC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Yijiang is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Yijiang's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Yijiang is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Yijiang is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Yijiang wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Yijiang, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Yile has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
yile_xiong.roast:
