Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yile plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yile has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yile showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U17, which means Yile traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Yile entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Yile paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yile keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yile has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yile, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yile. We will be here, taking notes.

A California competitor, Yinuo Gan is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 10 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Yinuo is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Yinuo has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, SoCal, Yinuo isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yinuo's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yinuo_gan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yinuo Gan, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. And yet, Yinuo keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

yixi_ziva_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yixi Ziva Liu, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 wins across 5 entries in 2 tournaments. That is a 40% conversion rate, which means roughly 6 out of every 10 times Yixi shows up to a tournament, Yixi pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Yixi saga began at 2023 South CRC, where Yixi burst onto the scene with a title in XD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Yixi has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. yiyang_jia.name: Yiyang Jia
yiyang_jia.summary:
Out of Washington, Yiyang Jia is a tenacious competitor with 3 quarterfinal-or-better finishes, currently competing at the U17 level, building a strong foundation across 7 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

A true triple-threat, Yiyang competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Yiyang has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Hanwen Yuan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Linda Zhao (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Yiyang has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Yiyang has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yiyang's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yiyang_jia.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yiyang Jia, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yiyang has 20 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yiyang saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yiyang plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Yiyang has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Hanwen Yuan: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Hanwen Yuan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15 — we hope Sophia Wu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Yiyang has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yiyang is not just losing locally — Yiyang is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jia family travels cross-country so Yiyang can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yiyang has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yiyang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U15, which means Yiyang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Yiyang entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Yiyang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yiyang keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yiyang has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yiyang, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yiyang. We will be here, taking notes.

yiyang_xu.name: Yiyang Xu
yiyang_xu.summary:
Hailing from New York, Yiyang Xu broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 26 entries across 12 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Yiyang has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Yiyang is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Yiyang has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Michael Li (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD
  • Justin Liu — a trusted BD partner (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Yiyang is still ascending.

Yiyang has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Yiyang isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Yiyang is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
yiyang_xu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yiyang Xu, from New York, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 win across 26 entries in 12 tournaments. That is a 4% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Yiyang shows up to a tournament, Yiyang pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Yiyang saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 Wayside NE CRC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yiyang's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yiyang plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Yiyang is 0-for-12. Zero titles in 12 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Yiyang and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Yiyang needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Yiyang has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yiyang's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Justin Liu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Justin Liu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U15 — we hope Vrishin Telikuntla has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yiyang's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Yiyang has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Yiyang has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yiyang has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yiyang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Yiyang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Yiyang entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Yiyang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 26 entries, and 25 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yiyang looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yiyang's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yiyang has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yiyang. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Lonely? Definitely.

yoning_liu.name: Yoning Liu
yoning_liu.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Yoning Liu has tasted victory multiple times, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 4 titles across 16 tournaments and 46 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Yoning keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Yoning has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Yoning is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Yoning has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Luna Han (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Saatvik B. Sunilraj — 3 events in XD, best: #2, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Yoning has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Yoning isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 4 titles, Yoning has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
yoning_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yoning Liu, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yoning has 46 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 9% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yoning saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 South OLC in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yoning's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Yoning actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Yoning is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yoning plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yoning can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Yoning has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yoning's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Grace Hu: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Grace Hu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Saatvik B. Sunilraj has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yoning's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Yoning has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Yoning has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Yoning has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yoning has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yoning showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17, which means Yoning traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Yoning entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Yoning paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 46 entries, and 42 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yoning looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yoning's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yoning has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yoning. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yooseung_jason_jung.name: Yooseung Jason Jung
yooseung_jason_jung.summary:
A California competitor, Yooseung Jason Jung has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 14 entries across 5 tournaments, Yooseung is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

What makes Yooseung especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Yooseung has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Shivani Ramakrishnan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Enming Cao (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Yooseung has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yooseung's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yooseung_jason_jung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yooseung Jason Jung, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Yooseung's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Yooseung keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Yooseung saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 3 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yooseung plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Yooseung has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Shivani Ramakrishnan (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U15 — we hope Zhenbang Yang has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yooseung has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yooseung showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15, which means Yooseung traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Yooseung entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Yooseung paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yooseung keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yooseung has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yooseung, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yooseung. We will be here, taking notes.

youshi_chen.name: Youshi Chen
youshi_chen.summary:
Out of California, Youshi Chen is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (BD U19) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 7 career titles across 26 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Notably, Youshi has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Youshi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 6 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (BD U19, 2024, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U19 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-3 (XD U17, BD U17) • 2 ORC titles.

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Youshi adapts to anyone:
  • Tian Qi Alan Zhang (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in BD/U19, across 2 seasons)
  • Tian Qi Zhang (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Zoey Enqi Tan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (4 events, best: #5)
  • Emily Liu — 3 events in XD, best: #3, across 2 seasons

Youshi is peaking at the right time in 2024-2025, with 6 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Youshi is still ascending.

Youshi has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Youshi has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 7 titles, Youshi has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
youshi_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Youshi Chen, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Youshi is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (BD U19), which means Youshi has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Youshi's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 62 career entries to discuss and a 11% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Youshi saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 6th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal CRC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Youshi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2024-2025 season has actually been the best yet — 6 titles in 11 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Youshi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Youshi has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Pan Am Junior, where Youshi entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BD U19, BD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Youshi does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Youshi has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 7 wins means Youshi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Youshi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Youshi is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Youshi and suddenly the wins start appearing — 6 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Youshi needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Youshi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Youshi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Audrey Chang: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Audrey Chang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U17 — we hope Stanley Xing has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has seen Youshi 6 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Youshi has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Youshi is not just losing locally — Youshi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Youshi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Youshi has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Youshi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Youshi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Youshi entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Youshi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 62 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Youshi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Youshi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Youshi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Youshi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yoyi_liao.roast:

Yoyi has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

yuancheng_li.name: Yuancheng Li
yuancheng_li.summary:
Out of North Carolina, Yuancheng Li is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 20 tournaments and 46 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Yuancheng keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Yuancheng is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Yuancheng is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

Having moved up to U17, Yuancheng is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Yuancheng brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Yuancheng has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Yuancheng isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 2 titles, Yuancheng has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
yuancheng_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yuancheng Li, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yuancheng has 46 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yuancheng saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yuancheng's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 20 entries — a stretch where Yuancheng actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Yuancheng is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yuancheng plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yuancheng can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Yuancheng has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yuancheng's texts. The go-to partner is Erela Liu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Gabby XZ Xiang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yuancheng's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Yuancheng has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Yuancheng of is playing it safe. Yuancheng has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Yuancheng has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Yuancheng has built a permanent residence on that line. Yuancheng has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yuancheng has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yuancheng showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Yuancheng traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Yuancheng entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Yuancheng paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 46 entries, and 44 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yuancheng looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yuancheng's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yuancheng has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yuancheng. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Yueran has 24 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yueran saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. Representing Illinois, Yuito Tao broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 14 entries across 6 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Yuito competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Anvi Sai Harish (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Rishi Jagannath (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Arunabh Ganguli (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2)

Yuito is peaking at the right time in 2022-2023, with 1 title across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Yuito is still ascending.

Yuito has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yuito's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yuito_tao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yuito Tao, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 14 entries in 6 tournaments. That is a 7% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Yuito shows up to a tournament, Yuito pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Yuito saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 Midwest OLC in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yuito's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yuito plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Yuito is 0-for-5. Zero titles in 5 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Yuito and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Yuito needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. The go-to partner is Anvi Sai Harish (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope Rishi Jagannath has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yuito's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. yuki_guan.name: Yuki Guan
yuki_guan.summary:
A California competitor, Yuki Guan has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Yuki's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Yuki is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Yuki competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 4 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Yuki has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 15 different players across the career:
  • Leslie Wang (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Siddhi Thorat (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 3 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Yuki is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Yuki has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Yuki has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 9 titles, Yuki has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
yuki_guan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yuki Guan, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 45 career entries in 16 tournaments, Yuki has 9 titles — a 20% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 4 entries where Yuki drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 45 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Yuki saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yuki's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 4 titles in 9 entries — a stretch where Yuki actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 3 wins in 10 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yuki plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yuki can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Yuki has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yuki's texts. The go-to partner is Joey Wang (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Jason Zhu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yuki's 9 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Yuki entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Yuki's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Yuki and said 'nah.' Tournament committees have handed Yuki a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Yuki has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in GS U13, where Yuki was seeded #1 and finished 9th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Yuki of is playing it safe. Yuki has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Yuki has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Yuki has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yuki is not just losing locally — Yuki is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 20% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Guan family travels cross-country so Yuki can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yuki has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yuki showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U13, which means Yuki traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event, where Yuki entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Yuki paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 45 entries, and 36 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yuki looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yuki's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yuki has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yuki. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Representing New York, Yuming Jack Peng is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 10 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Yuming has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Yuming brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

And yet, Yuming keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Yuming saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Yuming paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yuming keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yuming has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yuming, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yuming. We will be here, taking notes.

yunny_huang.name: Yunny Huang
yunny_huang.summary:
Out of Minnesota, Yunny Huang knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 12 entries across 4 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Yunny competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Yunny adapts to anyone:
  • Aaron Y Low (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Juliette Zhou (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)

The 2023-2024 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Yunny is still ascending.

Yunny has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Yunny brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
yunny_huang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yunny Huang, from Minnesota, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 12 entries in 4 tournaments. That is a 8% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Yunny shows up to a tournament, Yunny pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Yunny saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 Midwest CRC in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yunny's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yunny plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Yunny is 0-for-4. Zero titles in 4 tries. The best singles result is a 9th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Yunny and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Yunny needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Yunny has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U17 — we hope Prisha Kumari has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yunny's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yunny has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yunny showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Yunny traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Yunny entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Yunny paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 12 entries, and 11 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yunny looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yunny's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yunny has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yunny. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Notably, Yunqi has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Yunqi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Yunqi has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Ryan Chu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Yunqi has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yunqi's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yunqi_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yunqi Chen, from Washington, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yunqi has 11 career entries across 5 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yunqi saga began at 2022 NW Oregon OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Yunqi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Yunqi has reached the finals 2 times — at 2023 NW OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Yunqi has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yunqi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Yunqi has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Yunqi has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Yunqi has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yunqi has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yunqi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U11, which means Yunqi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-25 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Yunqi entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Yunqi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yunqi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yunqi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yunqi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yunqi. We will be here, taking notes.

The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Yuntian is right on schedule. The Yuntian saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Yuntian is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Yuntian, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

yushen_li.name: Yushen Li
yushen_li.summary:
Hailing from Washington, Yushen Li has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Yushen's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Yushen is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Yushen has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • George Shen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2)

Yushen has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yushen's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yushen_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yushen Li, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yushen has 16 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yushen saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yushen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Yushen has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is George Shen (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17 — we hope George Shen has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Yushen has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Yushen has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yushen has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yushen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Yushen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Yushen entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Yushen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yushen keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yushen has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yushen, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yushen. We will be here, taking notes.

yuteng_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yuteng Wu, from parts unknown. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Yuteng is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (BS), which means Yuteng has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Yuteng's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. Yuteng represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 1 career entries and a 0% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 1 entries worth of material.

And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Yuvan entered 2 events with results ranging from 65th to 65th. That is an average placement of 65th, which is a polite way of saying Yuvan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 29 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Yuvan. Yuvan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Yuvan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Yuvan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Yuvan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

yuwen_qu.name: Yuwen Qu
yuwen_qu.summary: Yuwen Qu has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U11 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 1 entries across 1 tournaments, Yuwen is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.
yuwen_qu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yuwen Qu, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Yuwen is right on schedule. It all started at 2026 Seattle NW CRC, and so far, the only thing Yuwen has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Yuwen saga began at 2026 Seattle NW CRC with 1 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Yuwen is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Yuwen's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Yuwen is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Yuwen is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Yuwen wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Yuwen, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

yuxuan_ariel_ng.name: Yuxuan Ariel Ng
yuxuan_ariel_ng.summary:
Yuxuan Ariel Ng is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U17 level, with 8 entries across 3 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Yuxuan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
