yuxuan_lin.name: Yuxuan Lin
yuxuan_lin.summary:
Representing Washington, Yuxuan Lin stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U17 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

What makes Yuxuan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #3, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 1 ORC title.

Yuxuan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • David Zuo (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Kanyanat Vajworarat (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Stanley Xing (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Alex He (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD

Yuxuan has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Yuxuan has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Yuxuan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yuxuan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yuxuan_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yuxuan Lin, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yuxuan has 35 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 9% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yuxuan saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Yuxuan burst onto the scene with a title in GS U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Yuxuan has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Yuxuan promised and what Yuxuan has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Yuxuan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 18 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yuxuan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yuxuan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Yuxuan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yuxuan's texts. The go-to partner is David Zuo (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15 — we hope Youshi Chen has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Yuxuan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Tournament committees have handed Yuxuan a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Yuxuan has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U15, where Yuxuan was seeded #2 and finished 33rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yuxuan is not just losing locally — Yuxuan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lin family travels cross-country so Yuxuan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yuxuan has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yuxuan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U15, which means Yuxuan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Yuxuan entered 3 events with results ranging from 3rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Yuxuan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 35 entries, and 32 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yuxuan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yuxuan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yuxuan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yuxuan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

yuxuan_zeng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yuxuan Zeng, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-5. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yuxuan has 61 career entries across 23 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yuxuan saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 MassBad NE OLC in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yuxuan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Yuxuan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Yuxuan is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Yuxuan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Yuxuan has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Yuxuan does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Yuxuan has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 4 wins means Yuxuan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yuxuan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Yuxuan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Yuxuan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yuxuan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Zhenxiang Deng: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Zhenxiang Deng has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Matthew Lei has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yuxuan's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Yuxuan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Yuxuan has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yuxuan is not just losing locally — Yuxuan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zeng family travels cross-country so Yuxuan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yuxuan has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yuxuan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17, which means Yuxuan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Yuxuan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Yuxuan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 61 entries, and 57 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yuxuan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yuxuan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yuxuan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yuxuan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

It all started at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC, and so far, the only thing Yves has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Yves saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yves plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yves has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yves showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in BS U17, which means Yves traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Dave Freeman OLC, where Yves entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Yves paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Yves is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Yves wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Yves, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

yves_judson_homayoon.name: Yves Judson Homayoon
yves_judson_homayoon.summary:
Representing California, Yves Judson Homayoon is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U19 level, with 16 entries across 7 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Yves competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Yves has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Kameran Hossain (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Yves has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, SoCal, Yves isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Yves brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
yves_judson_homayoon.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yves Judson Homayoon, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Yves has 16 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Yves saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yves plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. The longest-running partnership is with Kameran Hossain: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Kameran Hossain has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Kameran Hossain has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Yves has shown up 2 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Yves has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yves has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yves showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Yves traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Yves entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Yves paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Yves keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Yves has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Yves, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Yves. We will be here, taking notes.

yvonne_chen_yang.name: Yvonne Chen Yang
yvonne_chen_yang.summary:
A Washington competitor, Yvonne Chen Yang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Yvonne's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Yvonne has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Yvonne is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-2 finisher.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Yvonne adapts to anyone:
  • Owen Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (7 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Elina Xiao-En Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #2)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Yvonne is still ascending.

Yvonne has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Yvonne has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Yvonne's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
yvonne_chen_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Yvonne Chen Yang, from Washington, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 26 entries in 9 tournaments. That is a 4% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Yvonne shows up to a tournament, Yvonne pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Yvonne saga began at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2026 Seattle NW CRC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Yvonne's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Yvonne would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Yvonne has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Yvonne has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Yvonne's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Yvonne plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Yvonne is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Yvonne has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Yvonne's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Owen Wang: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Owen Wang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Owen Wang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Yvonne's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Yvonne has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Yvonne is not just losing locally — Yvonne is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Yvonne can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Yvonne has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Yvonne showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U11, which means Yvonne traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Yvonne entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Yvonne paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 26 entries, and 25 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Yvonne looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Yvonne's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Yvonne has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Yvonne. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
